speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Sidney Finkel. This is tape number four. OK. Mr. Finkel, when we broke you were describing life in the ghetto after the Aktion. Would you like to continue on that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was-- I-- you know, a lot of what I'm saying here is not-- is not-- some memory and some reconstruction of thoughts. It's that there were supposedly 2,000 legal people, and it was a little ghetto. And there was a list, a list of people that were allowed to stay behind. And, and I already described what happened to the illegal people. There was tremendous atrocities committed then. Most of these people were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some of the-- it came down-- I think this is important. It came down really to the 2,000 people. Many of them were the Jewish council, the Jewish police, and their families, and some workers. So I suspect that among those 2,000 people, they were mostly people of importance, you know what I mean? For a town before the war. I don't know how we fitted into that. I have no idea, really.

speaker: interviewee
text: But eventually the Germans decided that there was going to be-- there's no more ghetto. The, the people who were allowed to stay behind to live were either at the glass working factory or at Bugaj. The largest number were called Bugaj, which was around 1,000 Jews were in Bugaj. So this was a working camp where we lived on the premises. Now I should say that after this was done the Germans hung a sign at the railroad station that said Judenfrei, means no more Jews. Piotrkow is free of Jews. OK. So we move now to this Bugaj, which was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm with my father. The only one that is left that I'm aware of is my father, my brother, and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Lola, you weren't sure what happened to at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no idea what happened to Lola. I didn't even--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father or brother didn't know either?

speaker: interviewee
text: They probably did. But I didn't know. I didn't know explicitly even at that point what happened to my sister or anybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know about your mother at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that she wasn't with us. And I probably was aware that she was deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't necessarily know she was dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't know that she was dead. But I must-- I think I must have known because I never-- I never had any expectations of seeing them again. It was just something I never imagined. So that was kind of reconciled, that I wouldn't see them. So I probably knew. I would think that I knew. I was a pretty smart kid. I probably knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: About Ronia too, did you feel that way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ronia, your mother, and Frania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I think-- I think at that time I kind of shut my feelings off completely, and I really didn't concern myself with anybody or anything but myself. I was living in my own world.

speaker: interviewer
text: That came automatically, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I think so. I think that came automatically. So we are in this big work-- woodworking plant and we're making plywood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bugaj is B-U-G-A-J, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-J, right. It was by a river, it was called Bugaj. And there was a-- the plant was owned by two Germans, Dietrich and Fischer. I think Dietrich and Fischer. And they were quite cruel people. And they wouldn't think nothing of killing people and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that far from Piotrkow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, it was not. It was not. It was just almost adjoining it, I believe. Just a very short distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. We were living behind barbed wire. This, this whole factory was fenced in and was patrolled. It had a garrison of Polish policemen on there, some Ukrainians-- a lot of Ukrainians, actually. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At first I was working at some kind of machine, some woodworking machine or something like that, I remember working. But after a while I don't remember actually doing any work, any physical work. So I would just-- I do remember wandering around the place. I would watch-- they had a firing range on the-- on there. And I would remember these German visiting high ranking officers would come over, and they would be shooting. And I would just be very close, you know, far away, but I could see them. And I just admired them, saw that they were firing rifles and revolvers and things like that. There was a lot of Russian-- Ukrainians, which were like Russians with their horses, and they would do all kinds of antics on their horses. They would get drunk, and they would be galloping around the camp. I don't remember really much danger to me. I just remember kind of being free. I know that-- the only one I can remember from Bugaj was my, my cousin, and my cousin Anna and her two little girls. And I remember visiting them. They were in a different building. And the other significant thing that happened there to me was that my brother and a friend of his were engaged in smuggling in food. A lot of the transports that used to come in bringing in raw material, he was able to-- they were able to smuggle in bread and potatoes and stuff like that. And I just wanted to be of some use. And I always bugged him to help me, that I should be part of it. So, so he finally let me-- let me go with him where they were going to meet this wagon that was coming in, and we would carry some potatoes and stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when we were there-- him, myself, and somebody else-- this Fischer, this, this owner, he owned the-- he was also part of things, the Gestapo-- who owned this factory-- they hid some place and I was trying to hid. But anyway, I got caught. He caught me. He had his dog with him. He takes out his revolver, and he says-- he takes out a bullet and he shows, see this bullet here? I'm going to-- this is what is going to go through your head.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't know. I don't remember. Probably there was some conversation, I don't know what else, but I have no explanation for it, but he turned me over to the local police, which was the police, the Polish police, that was garrisoned in this factory. And there was a sergeant there, a police sergeant, that I knew from, I think from before the war or he knew my family. And I thought I would get off easy. And I was kind of talking to him, but I saw it wasn't doing any good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I looked him in the eyes and I says, my ass is not made out of glass. Go ahead and beat me. And they did. But it wasn't-- you know, maybe, 10, 15 lashes with a whip. But like I tell my, my kids when I talk at schools, I tell them that it was worth it because I was a hero. The word got out in the camp that I had defied them, and, and I was a hero for a day. Was your brother caught also at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My brother got away, his friend got away, I'm the only one who got caught. I think it was my first try, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your first what?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first attempt to smuggle stuff in. I think it was my first attempt. Maybe it wasn't, but I think it was. That's the only one I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember having a lot of fear when Fischer pulled out his gun?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You weren't afraid at that point? You were kind of numb?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if it was numb or not, I just didn't have any fear. I really didn't care.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel that way through most of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that helped you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe. There was something about my defiance that I think-- I think-- did something to these people that-- like this Fischer and so forth that they did not shoot me right then and there. There was something very defiant about me I think, but I could be wrong. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You think that's a trait you had even before the war? It just was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --born into you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Or the other way of looking at it is I was kind of dumb, you know? I just didn't know any fear because I could not associate with fear, you know, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happens next?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Then we-- you know, we're in this place about two years, which is the longest time outside of the ghetto that we're there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two years? Boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father and brother were still there with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father and brother are there. And then in November of 1944--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to talk any more about Bugaj? Any other major things happen before we go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember much anything more. I just-- oh, I do remember that my brother-in-law was there. Yeah. I do remember that he was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ronia's husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remember his name now?

speaker: interviewee
text: But, you know, it wasn't a bad-- no. But it wasn't-- to me, I don't think it ever sunk into me that this was very dangerous or I was being deprived of anything. I didn't-- I don't remember being really hungry, too hungry. I don't-- I remember sleeping on a cot with a lot of other people, you know. And I remember the sanitary conditions were pretty bad. And I remember wandering around the camp getting on one of these stacks of wood and things like that, looking outside the fence and seeing people walking in the field. I remember a young couple really, you know, walking the fields, like a boyfriend and girlfriend. And I thought-- the birds flying over and I thought, oh, it must be so wonderful to be free, to be outside and not fenced in into this ghetto, you know, this fence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever try to escape it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-mm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people escape it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to my knowledge. Not to my knowledge.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the-- was food served to you or did each individual unit cook their own food? How did that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I asked my cousin who is three years younger than me. She remembers an awful lot more than I do. That's Rita. She says there was a communal kitchen. And even there I suppose if you-- some people still managed to have some extra possessions or money or something. And they could buy additional food. People like, you know-- her father was a diamond-- important diamond merchant. And diamond is-- I don't know how, but they were able to put in their mouths or something like that, and they were able to still possess something to, to trade with. And I have an idea there were some Poles that brought in stuff for us or send in stuff somehow for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Plus the smuggling? Your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I think so, although I've never heard it said that it was so. I just have a feeling that there was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever a time at Bugaj when any of you, your father, your brother became very sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to my knowledge. But what continued to happen-- now I just remembered something-- is that there were a number of young children in addition to me in that camp. And that every once in a while the Germans would decide to cut the numbers down. And there would be a selection again. And we-- all the children would have to hide until it was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: A selection just for children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. For grownups.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just for-- OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I think for those to be sent to different camps and things like that. You know, the quota wasn't filled or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. In 19-- November, I think, of 1944, the Russians were very close to coming in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sense the-- did you have any knowledge of what was going on in the war at that time? Or did any of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I honestly did not know. I was-- I was really in a world of my own. I was literally living in a world of my own. Although I probably would have been old enough to understand and know things, I didn't. I just wasn't concerned about what was going on any place else. I wasn't concerned about other people. I was just by myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just kept to yourself. You didn't really have many friends there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. It's amazing when I think back on it because there were other young people there. I did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sleep in the same room as your father and brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, yeah. I think we slept in one communal type of bunkers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the room like? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just a big, big room with bunker beds-- not really bunker beds, more like bunkered, put together. What would you call it? A lot of people sleeping across.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like benches?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Something like that. But you know, a different level, different heights.

speaker: interviewer
text: Similar to the ones that we spoke about earlier that you would see at the Holocaust Museum, like Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They weren't that bad. There was a little bit-- they weren't that crowded. There was maybe like four people to a place or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have mattresses on them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they were straw mattresses. We had straw. And the Jewish community even then was quite organized. See, it's the wonderful thing about it. They were quite organized. So they had the cemetery people, and they had the government people so that the community would, would carry on. There would be some discipline. And I think there was some entertainment even provided.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other atrocities there that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- there were-- there were some atrocities. But the only thing I can go by is what my brother told me. And I know that he was on a work detail outside the camp. And some Polish woman claimed that the Jews, Jewish prisoners there stole a dress. And as soon as she told this to the Germans, the Germans lined them up, and they were going to shoot them. And my brother tells, you know, the story that he was just-- he's faced against a wall, and he was just laughing. He was just laughing because the whole situation was so ludicrous. And some Pole came along and talked to the woman, and they never stole some dress. And talked to them, the woman, and then she finally told him not to, that they didn't steal it. But they were going to be shot. My brother did-- was trying to make contact during that time and before with the underground. And they, according to him, they were-- they had no interest in any Jews joining the partisans. They had no interest. The only one that had any kind of a lukewarm interest were the communists of the different partisan political groups. But the main one, the Christian ones, did not want the Jews at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no help. There was nothing-- no help from the outside. So the very thing of escape, I think that, yes, they were thinking a lot about escaping. But escape to where and what? Because the Polish population was so hostile that they would love nothing better than to turn you in. Admittedly, it was very dangerous for them, too. They did not have it easy either, you know? To harbor a Jew or anything like that did mean instant death. OK. So what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Russians are-- the Russians are very close to where we-- where we were at.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, I think so. You know, I knew it from the attitude of the Ukrainians and the soldiers there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were fearful. They were very fearful. They were getting drunk and drunker every day because they knew that their punishment was going to be more severe than anybody's because they were really Russians. And the Russians were not Americans. They were deal-- dealt with them the same ways the Germans would, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Jewish population-- was there a sense-- do you remember a sense of excitement that the Russians were getting closer?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that. Because I don't remember-- this is-- this is the thing that I've been struggling with, is that I know from talking to my cousin and others who have survived the camp with me, that in 19-- November of 1944, which is the end of 1944, that the camp was evacuated. Now I have no memory of that whatsoever, except I remember the cattle cars waiting. That's where my memory wakes up. Until then, I don't remember anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't remember-- I was questioning my cousin, how did we get? Did we walk? How far was it from there to the railroad yard? And I don't remember any of that. But what I do remember is that the trains were waiting there. And I think it was during the night. And I didn't want to get in. I was separated from my father and my brother. I think I was separated. I think they violently threw me in because the Germans-- you know the Nazis, the SS, came for that purpose. And they threw me into this train. It was terribly crowded. You remember the name of the train station?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it Herby? H-E-R-B-Y?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So they threw you into the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Threw me into a train. And when those doors locked closed, it seems to me that there was a profound change in me at that point. From here on-- not from here on, but from in the older-- this was '44, so we're already like four years, maybe 4 and 1/2 years already under the German. And to me, it was still kind of an adventure, kind of a game. I didn't really realize the severity of all this and what it all meant. But I think the train, being in that cattle car, just-- I know I was very fearful, very depressed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the cattle car like, the conditions in there? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember it was terribly crowded and there wasn't any room to even lay down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any air coming in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I presume there was air coming. And I know it was during the night. And I remember the train moving and me just laying there, or sitting there, or standing up or whatever. I, I have no recollection of anybody else being there with me. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody you knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: That I knew. And yet I must have known everybody there. It was a very small community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know prior to leaving that-- was there a sense that Bugaj was emptying out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no memory. I have no memory of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just remember the cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I have no memory of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely not.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of clothes were you wearing at Bugaj? Did they-- were you wearing-- did they issue clothing to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I think we had the clothes from the ghetto. I had probably some trousers and some jacket. In November, it was very cold, but I don't even remember the coldness. Isn't it something? You think I would remember how cold it had-- Poland has very extreme temperatures. And I don't remember it even being cold or warm or anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you were wearing on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember it being cold on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you were on a train at night. What were you feeling?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was feeling very glum. I was feeling terribly depressed, which I realize now it was a great depression. I was totally cut off from everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had no idea what happened to your brother and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had no feelings. And I remember being-- before that I always had a lot of energy, a lot of adventure. And I was-- it was like I was totally withdrawn. Like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tired?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tired, yeah. And I just-- no hope. No-- none whatsoever. It was just-- it was just-- I think I just became-- at that time, I just became like on automatic reflexes. I would just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel like that was the lowest point in your life right there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it was-- that-- symbolically, that was the realization that something, what you might call my maturity. And I know I was terribly affected by that because two years ago when we went to Washington, DC with Leon and Ruthie and my wife Jean and some of their kids, and we went to the Holocaust Museum, I was able to go through-- navigate through everything until-- but when I saw those cattle truck, that's when I broke down. And for the first time probably in my life, I started to cry on my wife's shoulder. So that was-- symbolically, that is somehow extremely important in my psyche. And I realized that. It was like growing up and knowing that this was no longer fun and games. This was real. See, before then I kind of felt safe because I was who I was and my family, I thought, was prominent and wealthy. And that my father and my brother would protect me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You thought you were above the danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I had some magical--

speaker: interviewer
text: Power?

speaker: interviewee
text: --power or something that nothing could really happen to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on the train was the first time you really felt like--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Life is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Vulnerable. I began-- I felt really vulnerable on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your hope was dwindling?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't even have a hope.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no hope. It was just whatever comes next, I'll go along with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what came next? You're riding on the car at night. Do you remember daylight coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember daylight. I remember that we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that. I remember when we got off in a town called Czestochowa, which was a very much larger town then Piotrkow, but we were just in one section of it. We were in some kind of a camp. I mean, I think it was an ammunition factories in that camp. And those conditions then became really abominable. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: This still in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is still in Poland. You know, before, you know, we were talking about Piotrkow and Bugaj, we would think now those conditions were terrible, but they weren't compared to Czestochowa. The people there had been there for a very long time. There was no organization there. People there were very, well, maybe evil in the sense that nobody cared for anybody else. It was everybody's the strongest for themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the Jews felt that way too?

speaker: interviewee
text: The inmates, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The inmates and the--

speaker: interviewee
text: People were not human.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a factory setup, too? Just similar--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And the people-- their faces were yellow. A lot of the people's faces were yellow because they worked in the ammunition factory. And I guess the gunpowder or whatever, the chemicals made them yellow. And they-- as soon as their skin became yellow or they would soon die, or they would be shot, or something. And I just remember walking around. Again, I was not assigned a job or anything else. And at that time I was like 13 just walking around looking at the Germans and everything else. And the thing I remember most about that place-- and I know I wouldn't have survived there-- It was that if you went to the outhouse there--

speaker: interviewer
text: The what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outhouse. Washroom. I don't want to use the word washroom because it wasn't a washroom. It was just a block house, wooden place. And it was covered literally with shit. I mean the walls, the floors, everything was covered was shit. And I had-- my head was full of lice. I mean, they were everywhere on me. You just-- you couldn't clean. There was no sanitary condition period. There was nothing. And I'm sure that it would have been just the matter of a week or two, and I wouldn't have been able to survive because I don't think the food was anything there. And I don't remember seeing my brother or my father there, although they probably were. But I don't remember them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just remember being alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And just a few weeks-- we were only there a couple of weeks. And this was a very important town in Poland. And the Russian army was there right on the heel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't know that. Now again, I blank out with memory. We were packed into trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember sleeping there? Do you remember where you slept in Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just remember the vermin, the lice, and the unsanitary conditions? The food was-- what was the food like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: No memory?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no memory. Probably was soup and some salt, bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was just a few weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was just-- it was just the atmosphere there. It was just the, the helplessness of the other inmates. I don't think inmates is the right word.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember seeing people beaten or killed in Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I don't remember anybody. But I remember that they would just drop down and the next morning they would die. And they just be taken out, corpses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody was just dying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was just-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was really hell. And but I don't remember doing any work or anything. I just remember walking around the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: For a few weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we were-- again, I don't have any memory of it. All I remember-- I don't remember us getting into the train and going anyplace. But I do remember that we, after some kind of a ride, we come in. Must have been a long ride because I think Buchenwald is in Germany. It's quite a long way. Although I think Czestochowa is on the border of Germany. Coming into-- I remember coming into a place called Buchenwald into an-- like the train came in like in an underground tunnel or something. And that we came in there and we weren't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this was now?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, well, this had to be probably December of '44. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The end of '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember why you were sent from Czestochowa to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Only because the Russians were there. And they had orders, the Germans had orders to--

speaker: interviewer
text: So basically everybody was sent from that camp. They were cleaning it out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.
